title: When spaces are not enough
testCase: Did you turn the data into a real table?
blocks.0.text: Three bikes, three things about each: model, gears, price. You lined them up in columns with spaces. In the editor it looks perfect — everything is aligned.
blocks.2.text: A mess. Everything is glued onto one line. The spaces are gone. You know this already — the browser swallows both spaces and blank lines.
blocks.3.text:
Except this time

and
will not save you. Try it: put a
at the end of each line. The rows separate. And the columns? Gone. "Black" is a long word, "Red" is short, and everything drifts out of line.
blocks.4.text: You do not have a row problem. You have a COLUMN problem.
blocks.6.text: Everything you have written so far has been linear: one thing after another. A paragraph, then a paragraph. A bullet, then a bullet. Even a list goes one way — down.
blocks.7.text: This is different. "12 leva" does not belong only to the row "Red". It belongs ALSO to the column "Price". Every cell has two addresses: which row and which column. That cannot be said with tags lined up one after another.
blocks.9.items.2: <td> — one cell inside the row (table data)
blocks.11.text: Notice: there is no "column" tag. Columns happen on their own. You state only the rows and the cells inside them — and the browser sees that every row has three cells, and lines them up. A column is a consequence, not a command.
blocks.13.text: There. The columns line up, whatever you put in the cells. A long word, a short word — the table adjusts. You did not state a single width.
blocks.14.text: In the preview above I added borders so the grid shows. Your table will have none — borders are CSS. But the structure will be the same.
blocks.15.text: Watch the order of the tags
blocks.16.text: This trips people up constantly: <td> goes inside <tr>, not the other way round. A row contains cells; a cell does not contain a row. Get it backwards and the browser will quietly build something odd and you will wonder why.
blocks.17.text: And: every row must have the same number of cells. Forget one <td> and the table skews — everything after it shifts one column to the left.
blocks.18.text: Your turn. Turn the data into a real table. The first row holds the column names — leave it as an ordinary row for now, the next lesson will fix that.
